correctness.justification: All 24 acceptance tests pass. The implementation correctly handles valid emails (simple, subdomain, plus tags, special chars, numeric, hyphens, length boundary) and invalid emails (structural issues, local part rules, domain part rules, length limits). Every specified requirement is met.

edge_cases.justification: All edge cases pass: empty string, no '@', multiple '@', empty local part, domain without dot, leading/trailing dots in local and domain, consecutive dots, invalid characters, leading/trailing hyphens in domain labels, single-char TLD, numeric TLD, and the 254-character boundary condition are all handled correctly.

code_quality.justification: Clean, well-structured code with full type hints, descriptive naming, and good separation of concerns (validate_email, _validate_local, _validate_domain). Module-level compiled regex constants are a nice touch for performance. Minor point: the helper functions are module-private (prefixed with _) which is appropriate. Code is idiomatic Python throughout.

documentation.justification: Module-level docstring explains scope and RFC compliance limitations. Each function has a clear docstring with Args and Returns sections. The module constant MAX_EMAIL_LENGTH is commented. Inline comments explain the regex patterns. Only minor gap is no usage example in the module docstring.

test_coverage.justification: The test file is comprehensive: 7 valid email tests, 5 structural invalid tests, 5 local part invalid tests, 6 domain part invalid tests, and 1 length edge case test. Tests are well-named and clearly categorized with comments. The 254-char boundary test is particularly thorough. Minor gap: no test for local part at max length (64 chars) or domain label length limits.

robustness.justification: Input validation is solid: checks for empty string, length limit, exactly one '@', non-empty local and domain parts, and validates each component with compiled regexes. The function gracefully returns False for all invalid inputs rather than raising exceptions, which is appropriate for a validation utility. No crashes on bad input. Minor gap: no explicit handling of non-string inputs (e.g., None would raise AttributeError on split).
